title: DeBriefed 26 June 2026: Heat records broken across Europe | London climate action week | Introducing 'Project Cosmos' - Carbon Brief
gist: Europe experienced record-breaking heat in late June 2026, with France recording its hottest day ever and the UK breaking its June temperature record twice, linked by rapid attribution science to fossil-fuelled climate change. A London summit saw dozens of governments commit to rapid electrification, while Carbon Brief launched Project Cosmos — a 1.8 million-paper climate science database with a ranking of the 500 most-cited authors, revealing that US institutions dominate but face threat from the Trump administration.

themes.theme: European Record Heat and Climate Attribution

insights.claim: The UK broke its June temperature record twice in one week in late June 2026, with temperatures reaching 36.7C in Somerset, while France recorded its hottest day ever at 44.3C in Pissos on the same week.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Record temperatures are increasing demand shocks on energy and healthcare infrastructure, signalling accelerating physical climate risk for European asset holders.
insights.quote: temperatures reached 36.7C in Somerset on Thursday, as the "London Ambulance Service had its busiest-ever day for life-threatening emergencies"

insights.claim: Spain recorded its highest daily average temperature for June 2026 and Switzerland recorded its hottest June day at 37C in four locations in the same week, demonstrating the pan-European scale of the 2026 heat event.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Simultaneous multi-country records indicate a systemic shift rather than localised anomaly, raising the probability of repeated extreme heat events across portfolios exposed to European physical assets.
insights.quote: Spain also recorded its highest daily average temperature for June, said BBC News. On Thursday, Switzerland also had its hottest June day, when temperatures reached 37C in four locations

insights.claim: World Weather Attribution's rapid analysis found that fossil-fuelled climate change has made the June 2026 European heatwave the most severe and widespread in Europe's history.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Rapid attribution science is increasingly making the legal and reputational case for holding fossil fuel producers liable for climate damages in real time.
insights.quote: fossil-fuelled climate change has made this heatwave the most severe and widespread in Europe's history

insights.claim: Heat-related livestock deaths in England and Wales during summer 2025 doubled year-over-year to 6,600 animals, signalling rising agricultural losses from heat stress.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Doubling of livestock deaths in a single year indicates accelerating physical climate costs for agricultural and food supply chains, relevant to commodity and agri-food investors.
insights.quote: The number of livestock that perished in transport during heat in England and Wales from June to August 2025, double the number killed the year before

insights.claim: Dozens of governments led by the EU and the UK committed at London's Mansion House to accelerating electrification of homes, industry, and transport, explicitly aiming to curb demand for oil, coal, and gas.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A coordinated multi-government electrification commitment at ministerial level increases policy risk for fossil fuel demand projections and boosts the investability of clean electricity infrastructure.
insights.quote: calling for faster action to curb demand for oil, coal and gas by powering homes, industry and transport with clean electricity

insights.claim: The COP31 presidency is calling for countries to back a global goal of 35% of 'final' energy to come from electricity by 2035, with the UK's Climate Change Committee identifying electrification as the fastest path to lower energy bills and stronger energy security.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A 35%-by-2035 global electrification target, if adopted at COP31, would represent a binding catalyst for grid infrastructure, EV, and heat pump investment globally.
insights.quote: COP31 presidency calling for countries to back a global goal for 35% of "final" energy to come from electricity by 2035

insights.claim: The UK's Climate Change Committee's latest progress report uses the word 'electrification' far more frequently than previous editions, signalling an unprecedented policy emphasis on electrification as the core decarbonisation pathway.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Increased regulatory focus on electrification in UK advisory documents foreshadows future mandates and subsidies, creating durable tailwinds for British grid and clean energy companies.
insights.quote: The text of the CCC's latest report uses the word "electrification" far more often than previous editions

insights.claim: The UK parliament has approved its seventh carbon budget, targeting an 87% reduction in emissions below 1990 levels by 2040, a binding legislative milestone.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A legally binding 87%-by-2040 target locks in UK decarbonisation policy, providing long-duration regulatory certainty for infrastructure investors in UK energy and transport.
insights.quote: The UK parliament has approved its "seventh carbon budget", aimed at cutting emissions 87% below 1990 levels by 2040.

insights.claim: A French court ordered TotalEnergies to account for the emissions from the use (scope 3) of its products, following a case brought by a climate NGO, marking a significant expansion of corporate climate liability in Europe.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Scope 3 court-ordered accountability for TotalEnergies sets a legal precedent that could expose other European oil majors to similar liability, increasing litigation risk across the sector.
insights.quote: A French court has ordered oil-and-gas giant TotalEnergies to account for the emissions from the use of its products, following a case brought by a climate NGO

insights.claim: The US, Qatar, and other major energy exporters are pressuring the EU to rewrite planned methane emissions rules for oil-and-gas imports, citing potential disruption to European fuel supplies.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Coordinated producer-nation lobbying against EU methane import rules could delay or weaken the regulation, reducing the competitive disadvantage facing high-methane LNG suppliers to Europe.
insights.quote: The US, Qatar and other major energy exporters have urged the EU to "rewrite planned methane emissions" rules for oil-and-gas imports

insights.claim: Colombia's incoming fossil-fuel-supporting government — a Trump ally who defeated the current president — risks undermining progress from the April 2026 summit on transitioning away from fossil fuels that Colombia co-hosted.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Political reversals in key emerging-market fossil-fuel transition advocates create uncertainty for multilateral climate agreements and could slow momentum toward phasing down fossil fuel production.
insights.quote: It comes after the current Colombian government has been ousted by a presidential election defeat to a fossil-fuel-supporting Trump ally.

insights.claim: UN Secretary-General António Guterres called on AI companies to disclose their environmental impact and to source their power solely from renewables by 2030.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: UN-level pressure on AI companies for renewable-only power sourcing by 2030 is a precursor to potential regulatory mandates and reputational pressure on hyperscalers with high-carbon data centre footprints.
insights.quote: call on artificial intelligence companies to reveal their environmental impact and source their power solely from renewables by 2030

insights.claim: Guterres released a 'global call to action on methane' at London Climate Action Week, raising the political profile of methane reduction as a near-term climate lever.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Elevated UN focus on methane suggests increased regulatory and financial market scrutiny on oil-and-gas operators with high methane emission profiles.
insights.quote: Guterres also used his address to release a "global call to action on methane"

insights.claim: China's climate envoy Liu Zhenmin stated at the World Economic Forum that energy shortages from the Iran war should serve as a lesson for countries to accelerate energy transitions, positioning energy security as the primary rationale for clean energy adoption.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: China framing energy transition as energy security — rather than climate — at a major economic forum signals growing political consensus around clean energy adoption driven by geopolitical motives, broadening the investor base for energy transition assets.
insights.quote: energy shortages triggered by the Iran war should be a "lesson to countries to accelerate their energy transitions"

themes.theme: Project Cosmos: Climate Science Knowledge Infrastructure

insights.claim: Carbon Brief's Project Cosmos is the world's largest database of climate change research, comprising more than 1.8 million academic papers, books, and reports, built on the reference networks of all six IPCC assessment report cycles.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A comprehensive, publicly navigable database of climate science references enables faster identification of consensus, emerging risks, and knowledge gaps — a potential competitive tool for research-driven climate investors and policy analysts.
insights.quote: The database features more than 1.8m academic papers, books and reports, capturing the vast body of human knowledge about climate change that has accumulated over more than a century of academic study.

insights.claim: The IPCC's six assessment report cycles reference more than 100,000 other papers, books, and reports, forming the core citation network that Project Cosmos expanded using four additional data sources.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The 100,000-paper IPCC citation core, now networked to 1.8 million documents, means climate science literature can be systematically mined for emerging research signals in a way previously unavailable.
insights.quote: In total, IPCC reports reference more than 100,000 other papers, books and reports. This is the core of our climate science universe.

insights.claim: Prof Philippe Ciais is the most highly cited climate scientist in the Cosmos 500 ranking, with nearly four decades of research on the planet's carbon cycle.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Citation rankings provide a rapid shortlist of the most influential scientific voices on climate — relevant for investors assessing whose research is most likely to influence future IPCC reports and policy.
insights.quote: The most highly cited climate scientist is Prof Philippe Ciais, who has spent almost four decades researching the planet's carbon cycle

insights.claim: Almost half of the 500 most highly cited climate scientists (Cosmos 500) are from US institutions, but these researchers and institutions are now under attack from the Trump administration, raising concerns about the future of climate science leadership.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: US dominance of global climate research citations, combined with political suppression of US climate science, creates risk of a knowledge gap that could slow climate response and shift scientific leadership to other regions.
insights.quote: Almost half of the 500 most highly-cited authors are from US institutions. This raises particular concerns for the future of climate science, as US climate scientists and institutions are coming under attack under the Trump administration.

insights.claim: Experts from Global South countries account for only 4% of all authors in the Cosmos 500, and only 10% of Cosmos 500 authors are women, revealing deep structural inequalities in climate science authorship.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Underrepresentation of Global South researchers in high-citation climate science creates blind spots in understanding of climate impacts in the regions most vulnerable to them, a material risk for climate adaptation strategies.
insights.quote: Experts from global south countries account for only 4% of all authors in the Cosmos 500. China stands out as the most highly-cited global south country. Meanwhile, only 10% of authors in the Cosmos 500 are women.

themes.theme: Carbon Offset and Land-Use Emissions Research

insights.claim: New research in Nature finds that most carbon offset projects relying on 'avoided deforestation' have mixed, negligible, or negative impacts relative to control areas, undermining the credibility of this offset class.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Scientific invalidation of avoided-deforestation carbon credits by a top-tier journal accelerates regulatory and reputational risk for companies using these offsets to meet net-zero commitments.
insights.quote: Most carbon-offset projects relying on "avoided deforestation" have "mixed, negligible or negative impacts relative to control areas"

insights.claim: Research published in Nature projects that the strongest land-use carbon sinks to 2100 will be in China and Indonesia, while Brazil and the Democratic Republic of the Congo will dominate global carbon sources from land use.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Long-run divergence between carbon sink and source nations will shape where carbon border taxes, deforestation premiums, and nature-based investment flows concentrate over the next decades.
insights.quote: Projections of national land-use emissions to 2100 suggest the strongest "carbon sinks" will be in China and Indonesia, whereas Brazil and the Democratic Republic of the Congo will "dominate global sources"

insights.claim: Some world regions are now experiencing up to 50 additional heat stress days annually compared to 1950, according to research published in Nature Climate Change.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: A 50-day annual increase in heat stress days in certain regions dramatically raises physical risk for outdoor workers, agricultural productivity, and cooling energy demand — all material for sector-specific investment risk models.
insights.quote: Some world regions are experiencing up to 50 additional heat stress days annually, when compared to 1950

themes.theme: US Climate Science Under Political Threat

insights.claim: Former US government workers have recreated a valuable climate-science website that the Trump administration shut down last year, highlighting growing grassroots efforts to preserve US climate data infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Persistent efforts to preserve climate data outside government channels signal that US research disruption is real and ongoing, which may create longer-term gaps in publicly available climate monitoring data.
insights.quote: Former US government workers have "recreated a valuable climate-science website" shut down by the Trump administration last year
